subtopic_detail: Iron deficiency anaemia in toddler with excessive cow milk intake
stem: You are the GP reviewing a 22-month-old boy whose mother reports he tires easily on walks and is increasingly pale. He drinks about 750 mL of full-cream cow milk a day from a bottle and refuses meat, eggs and fortified cereal. He was exclusively breastfed until 10 months. Vaccinations are up to date. The family is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander. He has no overt bleeding, no jaundice and no chronic illness. The blood film shows microcytic hypochromic red cells with anisocytosis.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 °C, HR 116, RR 24, SpO2 99% room air, weight on 50th centile
data_table.Examination: Conjunctival and palmar pallor; cardiovascular and abdominal examination otherwise normal; no organomegaly
data_table.Investigations: Hb 84 g/L, MCV 64 fL, ferritin 6 micrograms/L, CRP 2 mg/L, reticulocytes low

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next management step?

options.text: Trial a multivitamin syrup for six weeks before considering targeted iron treatment
options.rationale: Multivitamin syrups contain too little elemental iron to correct established deficiency anaemia; a therapeutic iron dose is needed alongside dietary change. (Source: RCH CPG Iron deficiency)

options.text: Refer to paediatric haematology for an inpatient intravenous iron infusion
options.rationale: IV iron is reserved for failure of oral therapy, malabsorption or intolerance; uncomplicated nutritional deficiency responds to oral iron and dietary modification. (Source: RCH CPG Iron deficiency)

options.text: Oral elemental iron 3 mg/kg once daily and restrict cow milk to under 500 mL daily
options.rationale: Australian guidance recommends 3 mg/kg/day of elemental iron for at least 3 months in nutritional iron deficiency, plus cow milk volume below 500 mL to reduce iron antagonism. (Source: RCH CPG Iron deficiency)

options.text: Send haemoglobin electrophoresis to exclude thalassaemia before commencing treatment
options.rationale: The very low ferritin and dietary history give an unambiguous nutritional diagnosis; electrophoresis is reserved if the anaemia fails to respond to iron replacement. (Source: RCH CPG Iron deficiency)

options.text: Increase dietary iron alone and recheck the full blood count in eight weeks
options.rationale: Diet alone is too slow to correct symptomatic anaemia with Hb 84 g/L; oral iron replacement is required in addition to dietary advice. (Source: RCH CPG Iron deficiency)

explanation.summary: Excessive cow milk intake above 500 mL per day is a leading cause of toddler iron deficiency in Australia because milk displaces iron-rich foods and reduces non-haem iron absorption. The Royal Children's Hospital guideline recommends 3 mg/kg/day of elemental iron orally for at least 3 months, then a further 3 months once Hb normalises to replenish stores. A FBC and ferritin check at 4 to 6 weeks documents response. Dietary modification including red meat, iron-fortified cereals and vitamin C with meals is essential alongside supplementation.
key_points: Cow milk above 500 mL daily is a key dietary risk factor.
Elemental iron dose is 3 mg/kg once daily for three months.
Recheck FBC and ferritin at four to six weeks for response.
IV iron is reserved for oral failure, intolerance or malabsorption.
explanation.pearls: Expect a reticulocyte rise within 5 to 7 days and a Hb rise of about 10 g/L per month on oral iron; failure to respond after 4 weeks should prompt review of adherence, ongoing milk excess, or alternative diagnoses such as coeliac disease.

subtopic_detail: Atopic eczema stepped care in a toddler
stem: You are the GP seeing a 2-year-old girl with a 6-week history of dry itchy red flexural rashes at the antecubital and popliteal fossae, worse overnight and disturbing sleep. Her mother applies sorbolene cream twice a day. She has no infection signs and no oozing. Family history includes parental hay fever. The family is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander. Vaccinations are up to date. There is no relevant medication history.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.6 °C, HR 104, RR 22, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Lichenified erythematous plaques at flexures with excoriations; no honey-crust, no vesicles, no fever

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next management step?

options.text: Continue sorbolene only and review again in eight weeks without active treatment
options.rationale: Established lichenified eczema disturbing sleep needs active anti-inflammatory therapy; emollients alone are inadequate at this stage of the disease. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Dermatology)

options.text: Apply hydrocortisone 1% to the flexures twice daily plus a thick emollient liberally
options.rationale: Mild topical corticosteroid plus generous emollient is the stepped-care first-line for paediatric flexural atopic eczema, escalating potency if uncontrolled after a defined trial. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Dermatology)

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone for 7 days alongside the existing sorbolene moisturiser
options.rationale: Systemic corticosteroids are not recommended for routine paediatric eczema due to rebound flare and adverse effects; topical therapy should be optimised first. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Dermatology)

options.text: Start a daily non-sedating antihistamine without any change to topical treatment
options.rationale: Non-sedating antihistamines do not reduce eczema inflammation; the itch in eczema is histamine-independent and responds to topical steroid plus emollient. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Dermatology)

options.text: Refer urgently to dermatology for ciclosporin without a trial of topical steroid first
options.rationale: Systemic immunomodulators are reserved for severe disease unresponsive to optimised topical therapy; a stepwise trial must be completed first. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Dermatology)

explanation.summary: Australian guidance recommends a stepped-care approach to paediatric atopic eczema starting with generous emollient use plus a topical corticosteroid matched to severity and site. Mild potency steroids such as hydrocortisone 1% are appropriate for the face and flexures of young children, with stronger steroids reserved for thicker plaques or recalcitrant disease. A finger-tip unit guide helps families dose correctly. Antibiotic therapy is reserved for clinical infection rather than colonisation alone.
key_points: Use mild topical steroid for flexural toddler eczema.
Emollients applied liberally several times per day are essential.
Reserve systemic therapy for failure of optimised topical care.
Treat infected eczema with cefalexin or flucloxacillin if oozing or honey-crusted.
explanation.pearls: A single finger-tip unit (about 0.5 g) treats an area equal to two adult palms; under-dosing is the commonest reason for apparent topical steroid failure in toddler eczema.

subtopic_detail: Catch-up schedule for a partially vaccinated arrival
stem: You are the GP seeing a 14-month-old boy newly arrived from overseas. His written records confirm three doses of DTaP-IPV-Hib-HepB at 2, 4 and 6 months, but no further immunisations. He is well, has no immunocompromise and is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander. Mother asks what vaccines he should receive today to bring him up to schedule. Routine 12-month and 18-month doses are otherwise outstanding.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.5 °C, HR 110, RR 24, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Well thriving toddler; growth on 50th centile; cardiovascular and respiratory unremarkable
data_table.Investigations: Not required prior to catch-up immunisation

lead_in: Which combination of vaccines should be administered today?

options.text: DTPa-IPV booster only today and defer all other vaccines to the next scheduled visit
options.rationale: Catch-up should bring multiple antigens up to schedule at one visit; deferring leaves the child unprotected against MMR, MenACWY and pneumococcal disease. (Source: Australian Immunisation Handbook)

options.text: Defer immunisation until serology confirms current measles and varicella immune status
options.rationale: Catch-up schedules do not require pre-vaccination serology; serology delays protection and is not part of routine Australian practice. (Source: Australian Immunisation Handbook)

options.text: Hepatitis A vaccine and varicella vaccine only today and review again in four weeks
options.rationale: Hepatitis A is not on the universal schedule for non-Aboriginal children; varicella alone misses MMR, meningococcal and pneumococcal protection that are now due. (Source: Australian Immunisation Handbook)

options.text: MMR plus meningococcal ACWY plus pneumococcal 13-valent at this visit using catch-up
options.rationale: These are the routine 12-month doses on the Australian schedule and are due now; the 18-month doses are given separately at the next planned visit. (Source: Australian Immunisation Handbook)

options.text: Restart the schedule from infancy doses including DTPa, polio and hepatitis B today
options.rationale: Documented doses received overseas are accepted by the Australian Immunisation Handbook; restarting is unnecessary and causes vaccine wastage and overdosing. (Source: Australian Immunisation Handbook)

explanation.summary: The Australian Immunisation Handbook provides catch-up schedules that respect previously documented doses while bringing children rapidly up to age-appropriate cover. At 12 months MMR, meningococcal ACWY and 13-valent pneumococcal conjugate are due, and 18-month doses include MMRV, DTPa and Hib. Serology before catch-up is not required. Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children, and those with medical risk conditions, receive additional doses such as hepatitis A and pneumococcal polysaccharide where indicated.
key_points: Accept documented overseas doses; do not restart the schedule.
At 12 months give MMR, MenACWY and PCV13.
Serology is not required prior to catch-up immunisation.
Use the Immunise Australia online catch-up calculator for complex histories.
explanation.pearls: All live parenteral vaccines including MMR, varicella and MMRV can be co-administered on the same day; if not given together they must be separated by at least 4 weeks to avoid blunting the immune response.

subtopic_detail: Discrimination of ITP from HSP in school-age child with petechiae
stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a previously well 5-year-old girl with two days of widespread bruising and mucosal bleeding from the gums. She had a viral upper respiratory illness two weeks ago. She has no fever, no joint swelling, no abdominal pain and no rash on the buttocks or legs. Examination shows scattered petechiae across the trunk and limbs with wet purpura on the buccal mucosa. Liver and spleen are not palpable. She is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 °C, HR 110, RR 22, SpO2 99% room air, BP 100/64
data_table.Examination: Wet purpura on buccal mucosa, scattered petechiae trunk and limbs; no hepatosplenomegaly; no lymphadenopathy
data_table.Investigations: Hb 122 g/L, WCC 7.4, platelets 8 x10^9/L, blood film normal red and white cells, large platelets

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Admit and treat with intravenous immunoglobulin plus close inpatient bleeding observation
options.rationale: Wet mucosal purpura with platelets under 10 x10^9/L is severe ITP with a real intracranial bleed risk; IVIG rapidly raises the platelet count and admission is mandatory. (Source: RCH CPG ITP)

options.text: Discharge home with planned platelet recheck in one week and oral tranexamic acid
options.rationale: Wet purpura is a marker of severe bleeding risk; outpatient management is not appropriate until the platelet count has been raised and bleeding controlled. (Source: RCH CPG ITP)

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 4 mg/kg/day and review again in clinic in seven days
options.rationale: Steroids are an alternative but slower-acting therapy and bone marrow aspirate is needed before high-dose steroid to exclude leukaemia in atypical presentations. (Source: RCH CPG ITP)

options.text: Order an urgent bone marrow aspirate prior to starting any specific drug therapy
options.rationale: Bone marrow examination is not required in a classic well child with isolated thrombocytopenia, normal blood film and no atypical features; it is reserved for atypical cases. (Source: RCH CPG ITP)

options.text: Transfuse one adult unit of platelets immediately and admit for routine observation only
options.rationale: Platelet transfusion is reserved for life-threatening bleeding only because autoantibodies destroy transfused platelets rapidly; IVIG or steroid is the standard therapy. (Source: RCH CPG ITP)

explanation.summary: Acute paediatric ITP typically presents 1 to 4 weeks after a viral illness with isolated thrombocytopenia and an otherwise well child. The Royal Children's Hospital guideline reserves active therapy for wet purpura, severe mucosal bleeding or planned procedures; IVIG raises platelets within 24 to 48 hours. Bone marrow examination is needed only when features are atypical, when steroid is being considered without prior marrow review, or when the response is poor. HSP is excluded clinically by the absence of palpable purpura, arthritis and abdominal pain.
key_points: Wet purpura plus platelets under 10 indicates severe ITP needing IVIG.
Platelet transfusion is reserved for catastrophic bleeding only.
Bone marrow is not required for classic acute paediatric ITP.
HSP is distinguished by palpable purpura on lower limbs and buttocks.
explanation.pearls: IVIG 0.8 g/kg as a single dose typically raises platelets above 50 x10^9/L within 48 hours; if no rise occurs by day 3, recheck the diagnosis with a bone marrow aspirate before further immunosuppression.

subtopic_detail: Undescended testis referral timing in a 9-month-old
stem: You are the GP reviewing a 9-month-old boy at his routine check. His mother says the right testis has never been visible in the scrotum. On examination, the right testis is palpable high in the inguinal canal and cannot be manipulated into the scrotum. The left testis is normally positioned. He was born at term and is otherwise well, with no abnormal genitalia, no hernia and no syndromic features. He is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.6 °C, HR 116, RR 28, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Right testis high in inguinal canal, non-retractable; left testis normal; phallus normal; no inguinal hernia
data_table.Investigations: Imaging not indicated; karyotype not indicated

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Reassure parents and review the testicular position again at his 24-month visit
options.rationale: Spontaneous descent after 6 months is uncommon and delayed surgery worsens fertility and increases later malignancy risk; review at 24 months is too late. (Source: RCH CPG Undescended testis)

options.text: Refer to paediatric surgery so that orchidopexy can be planned before 18 months of age
options.rationale: Persistent undescended testis after 6 months warrants surgical referral so that orchidopexy is performed before 18 months to preserve spermatogenesis and reduce malignancy risk. (Source: RCH CPG Undescended testis)

options.text: Arrange a scrotal and inguinal ultrasound to confirm the position prior to any referral
options.rationale: Imaging does not change management for a palpable testis and adds delay; surgical referral is based on clinical findings alone. (Source: RCH CPG Undescended testis)

options.text: Commence intramuscular human chorionic gonadotropin to encourage descent before surgery
options.rationale: Hormonal induction with hCG is not part of standard Australian care because of poor efficacy and adverse effects; surgical orchidopexy is the recommended approach. (Source: RCH CPG Undescended testis)

options.text: Send karyotype and androgen receptor studies before deciding on any specific surgery
options.rationale: Endocrine and genetic studies are reserved for bilateral non-palpable testes or ambiguous genitalia, not a single palpable undescended testis with normal phallus. (Source: RCH CPG Undescended testis)

explanation.summary: Australian guidance recommends orchidopexy between 6 and 18 months for an undescended testis because surgery before 18 months improves long-term fertility and reduces malignancy risk. Spontaneous descent is uncommon after 6 months, so referral at the 6 to 12-month check should not be delayed. Imaging adds no value for a palpable testis. Endocrine workup is reserved for bilateral non-palpable testes or any sign of disorders of sex development.
key_points: Refer to paediatric surgery once persistence is clear after 6 months.
Operate before 18 months to preserve fertility.
Imaging does not change management for a palpable testis.
Bilateral non-palpable testes warrant endocrine and genetic evaluation.
explanation.pearls: Bilateral non-palpable testes in a newborn are a disorder-of-sex-development emergency until proven otherwise; obtain electrolytes, 17-hydroxyprogesterone and karyotype before assigning sex of rearing.
